_comment: Quiz bank for Circular City. Keyed by event id. Each event has three difficulty tiers (easy/standard/hard); each tier has question, options, correctIndex (0-based), and optionExplanations (one per option: why the correct one is right, why each distractor is wrong). Edit here and re-run build_events to regenerate events.json. Teacher selects the live tier via config.quiz.difficulty.

easy.question: Which approach treats rubbish as a resource instead of just throwing it away?
options: Recycling and reducing waste
Burying everything in a tip
Throwing it in a river
Ignoring it
optionExplanations: Right: recycling and reducing keep materials in use — a circular city.
Wrong: burying discards materials — that's linear.
Wrong: river dumping pollutes and wastes materials.
Wrong: ignoring waste solves nothing.

standard.question: A council adopts a 'collect-and-bury' approach as its founding waste strategy. Which statement BEST explains why this is the least aligned with sustainable urban development?
options: It treats discarded material as a resource, closing the materials loop
It sustains a linear throughput of resources and externalises long-term costs onto future land, air and water
It is the most expensive option and immediately strains the municipal budget
It maximises citizen participation in waste reduction
optionExplanations: Wrong: closing the loop describes a circular metabolism; burying waste discards materials and is linear.
Correct: linear disposal keeps resource throughput high and shifts costs (leachate, methane, lost land) to the future and surrounding areas.
Wrong: disposal is the cheapest option up front; its costs are long-term and externalised, not an immediate budget hit.
Wrong: burial requires no behaviour change — participation is associated with reduce/recycle strategies, not disposal.

hard.question: A planner argues disposal-led management is 'most rational' for a fast-growing low-income city with cheap land. What is the strongest sustainability counter-argument?
options: Even with cheap land, disposal externalises rising leachate, methane and health costs that compound as the city grows
Disposal is more expensive than recycling from the very first day
Cheap land is never available in low-income cities
Disposal prevents any economic growth at all
optionExplanations: Correct: the costs are deferred and externalised, growing with the city — the core sustainability objection.
Wrong: disposal is cheapest up front; the issue is long-term cost.
Wrong: an overgeneralisation; such land often does exist.
Wrong: overstated — disposal does not preclude growth.

easy.question: Why is using less packaging better than recycling it?
options: Less waste is created in the first place
It makes more rubbish
It is completely free
Recycling is banned
optionExplanations: Right: prevented waste never needs handling.
Wrong: reducing means less waste, not more.
Wrong: it has some cost, but avoids waste.
Wrong: recycling isn't banned.

standard.question: Reduction sits above recycling in the waste hierarchy. Which reasoning BEST justifies that ranking?
options: Recycling is technically impossible for most packaging
Preventing waste avoids the energy, emissions and cost of later collecting, sorting and reprocessing it
Reduction generates more recyclable material to sell
Recycling inevitably contaminates groundwater
optionExplanations: Wrong: most packaging is recyclable; technical impossibility is not the basis of the hierarchy.
Correct: waste never created needs no downstream handling, so prevention avoids all those costs and impacts.
Wrong: reduction lowers the amount of material entering the stream — the opposite of generating more.
Wrong: leachate is a landfill issue, not an inherent feature of recycling.

hard.question: Critics call a packaging tax 'slow and ineffective'. Which response best defends it on sustainability grounds?
options: By internalising disposal costs it shifts producer and consumer behaviour toward less packaging, even if benefits lag
It immediately eliminates all packaging waste
It guarantees higher recycling revenue this year
It has no effect on behaviour
optionExplanations: Correct: pricing externalities changes behaviour over time — the lag is expected, not a refutation.
Wrong: overstated; change is gradual.
Wrong: it targets reduction, not short-term revenue.
Wrong: that contradicts the rationale for the tax.

easy.question: What do cheap goods 'made to be thrown away' do to a city's rubbish?
options: Increase it
Decrease it
Have no effect
Recycle it automatically
optionExplanations: Right: constant replacement piles up waste.
Wrong: they add waste, not reduce it.
Wrong: they clearly add to the bins.
Wrong: throwaway goods aren't auto-recycled.

standard.question: Why does a 'right to repair' law tackle the waste problem more fundamentally than simply expanding collection?
options: It extends product lifespans, reducing the volume of goods entering the waste stream
It increases manufacturing output to meet demand
It removes the need for any recycling system
It lowers collection costs without changing consumption
optionExplanations: Correct: durable, repairable goods are replaced less often, cutting waste at its structural source.
Wrong: it slows replacement demand, so it tends to reduce, not increase, output.
Wrong: it reduces but does not eliminate end-of-life waste; recycling is still needed.
Wrong: that describes a collection efficiency, not addressing the root driver (built-in obsolescence).

hard.question: Why might 'right to repair' regulation cut waste more durably than subsidising repair cafés?
options: It changes product design upstream, addressing the structural cause rather than only the symptom
Repair cafés increase manufacturing output
Regulation removes the need for any recycling
Subsidies always outperform regulation
optionExplanations: Correct: mandating repairable design tackles obsolescence at source, a more durable fix.
Wrong: cafés extend product life, not raise output.
Wrong: recycling is still needed.
Wrong: unsupported absolute claim.

easy.question: Why is takeaway packaging often hard to recycle?
options: It is dirty with food
It is made of gold
It is always spotless
It disappears by itself
optionExplanations: Right: food contamination makes much of it non-recyclable.
Wrong: it's cheap plastic, not gold.
Wrong: it's usually soiled.
Wrong: plastic does not vanish.

standard.question: The delivery surge raised plastic waste sharply. Why is this difficult to solve through recycling alone?
options: Food-contaminated single-use plastics are often non-recyclable, keeping capture rates low
Most delivery packaging is biodegradable
Recycling mixed plastics is always profitable
Delivery packaging is predominantly glass
optionExplanations: Correct: contamination by food makes much of this plastic uneconomic or impossible to recycle, so it leaks to disposal.
Wrong: the packaging is largely non-biodegradable plastic.
Wrong: mixed-plastic recycling is frequently loss-making and price-volatile.
Wrong: it is overwhelmingly plastic, not glass.

hard.question: A city adds recycling points for takeaway waste but capture stays low. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Food contamination makes much single-use plastic unrecyclable regardless of bin provision
Residents recycle too enthusiastically
Plastic is biodegradable, so it isn't collected
Recycling points reduce the waste generated
optionExplanations: Correct: contamination, not bin availability, is the binding constraint here.
Wrong: nonsensical as a cause of low capture.
Wrong: the plastic is non-biodegradable.
Wrong: conflates collection points with source reduction.

easy.question: Why set up recycling in a new neighbourhood from the start?
options: Habits are hard to change later
New areas make no waste
Fixing later is always cheaper
It removes the need for bins
optionExplanations: Right: early habits and infrastructure stick.
Wrong: occupied homes make waste at once.
Wrong: retrofitting is usually pricier.
Wrong: collection is still needed.

standard.question: Why is building waste infrastructure into a new district from the outset more sustainable than retrofitting later?
options: It locks in collection-and-disposal patterns and infrastructure that are costly to reverse once established
New districts generate no waste in their first years
Retrofitting is always cheaper than building new
It removes the need for any collection service
optionExplanations: Correct: path dependency means early infrastructure and habits shape behaviour for decades; changing them later is expensive.
Wrong: occupied homes generate waste immediately.
Wrong: retrofitting established systems is generally more expensive and disruptive.
Wrong: collection is still required regardless of design.

hard.question: Designing circular systems into a new district is defended on 'path dependency'. What does that mean?
options: Early infrastructure and habits become self-reinforcing, making later change costly
New districts are legally exempt from waste rules
Waste systems have no influence on behaviour
Costs always fall over time regardless of design
optionExplanations: Correct: initial choices lock in trajectories that are expensive to reverse.
Wrong: no such exemption exists.
Wrong: infrastructure shapes behaviour.
Wrong: retrofitting is typically costlier.

easy.question: Why is rubbish left uncollected in the streets dangerous?
options: It spreads disease and pests
It only looks untidy
It keeps people healthy
It has no effect
optionExplanations: Right: piles breed pests and pollute water.
Wrong: it's a health hazard, not just messy.
Wrong: it harms, not helps, health.
Wrong: it has serious effects.

standard.question: Uncollected waste in dense low-income districts is primarily a sustainability concern because it...
options: creates disease vectors and contaminates water, undermining the social and environmental pillars of SUD
directly lowers national GDP figures
is purely an aesthetic nuisance
raises recycling rates through informal picking
optionExplanations: Correct: accumulating waste breeds pests and pollutes water, harming health (social) and ecosystems (environmental).
Wrong: any GDP effect is indirect; the core concern is health and environmental harm.
Wrong: it is a serious health hazard, not merely unsightly.
Wrong: informal picking may occur, but it does not offset the public-health emergency and is not the primary concern.

hard.question: In low-income cities, which mechanism most directly turns uncollected waste into a public-health crisis?
options: Disease vectors breeding in waste while leachate contaminates water supplies
The aesthetic decline of neighbourhoods
Reduced property-tax revenue
Increased informal recycling activity
optionExplanations: Correct: vectors and water contamination are the direct disease pathways.
Wrong: appearance is secondary to health.
Wrong: a fiscal effect, not the health mechanism.
Wrong: a side-effect, not the cause of the crisis.

easy.question: Recycling bins only work well if...
options: people actually sort their waste
the bins are a nice colour
the city is rich
a landfill is nearby
optionExplanations: Right: sorting by residents is essential.
Wrong: colour barely matters.
Wrong: money alone can't force sorting.
Wrong: landfill distance is irrelevant.

standard.question: Heavily-funded source-separation schemes can still fail. Which factor is the decisive variable?
options: Citizen participation and low contamination, since mixed/contaminated streams cannot be economically reprocessed
The colour scheme of the bins
The size of the municipal budget on its own
Proximity of homes to the nearest landfill
optionExplanations: Correct: recycling economics depend on clean, well-sorted material; without participation, contamination collapses the system.
Wrong: bin design is marginal compared with sorting behaviour.
Wrong: money buys infrastructure but cannot force correct sorting — participation is the binding constraint.
Wrong: distance to landfill does not determine recycling success.

hard.question: Two cities invest equally in source separation; one recycles far more. The decisive difference is most likely...
options: participation and contamination levels, which determine how much material is truly recoverable
the brand of collection vehicles
proximity to the nearest incinerator
the size of the city's debt
optionExplanations: Correct: behaviour and material quality drive recovery once investment is equal.
Wrong: vehicle brand is immaterial.
Wrong: incinerator distance is irrelevant.
Wrong: debt does not determine recycling capture.

easy.question: Besides homes, what else creates a city's waste?
options: Factories and businesses
Only households
Nothing else
Rivers
optionExplanations: Right: industry and commerce produce lots of waste.
Wrong: homes aren't the only source.
Wrong: many sources exist.
Wrong: rivers aren't a waste source.

standard.question: Planning a city's waste system only around household rubbish is inadequate because...
options: industrial and commercial activity generate large, varied and sometimes hazardous streams the system must handle
households are the sole source of hazardous waste
industry produces no waste of its own
commercial waste is automatically recycled
optionExplanations: Correct: urban waste includes substantial industrial/commercial flows with different handling needs.
Wrong: industry, not households, is the main source of many hazardous wastes.
Wrong: industrial processes are significant waste generators.
Wrong: commercial waste is not recycled by default; it must be managed.

hard.question: Why can attracting industry boost the economy yet worsen the sustainability scorecard at once?
options: It adds revenue but also generates varied and hazardous waste the system must absorb
Industry produces no waste, so there is no trade-off
Economic growth always improves environmental indicators
Hazardous waste is automatically recycled
optionExplanations: Correct: economic and environmental indicators can move in opposite directions — a real trade-off.
Wrong: industry is a major waste source.
Wrong: growth and environment can conflict.
Wrong: hazardous waste needs deliberate handling.

easy.question: Why does throwing rubbish in a river harm faraway places?
options: It flows downstream and pollutes
It stays in one spot
Rivers clean all waste
It only affects fish prices
optionExplanations: Right: water carries pollution far away.
Wrong: it spreads, not stays.
Wrong: rivers carry, not clean, waste.
Wrong: harm is far broader than prices.

standard.question: 'Wrap-and-throw' river dumping shows how local mismanagement becomes a regional problem because...
options: leachate and waste travel downstream, contaminating water sources and coasts far from the source
rivers neutralise all contaminants they carry
the impact is confined to the exact dumping point
it only affects local fish-market prices
optionExplanations: Correct: flowing water transports pollutants, spreading contamination across the catchment and to the sea.
Wrong: rivers transport, not neutralise, most contaminants.
Wrong: the whole point is that effects spread well beyond the dumping site.
Wrong: the harm is ecological and public-health, far broader than fish prices.

hard.question: 'Wrap and throw' is best understood as a symptom of which underlying failure?
options: Absent sanitation and collection, leaving residents no managed alternative
Excessive recycling enthusiasm
Over-investment in landfills
A surplus of clean water
optionExplanations: Correct: it is a coping response to missing infrastructure.
Wrong: unrelated to recycling.
Wrong: the problem is too little infrastructure, not too much.
Wrong: water scarcity/quality is the consequence, not the cause.

easy.question: Why teach children about recycling?
options: It builds good habits for the future
Bins sort waste by themselves
It replaces all bins
Habits don't matter
optionExplanations: Right: participation depends on attitudes.
Wrong: bins don't sort themselves.
Wrong: education adds to, not replaces, infrastructure.
Wrong: habits strongly affect recycling.

standard.question: Waste education yields little in the current year. On what grounds is the investment still defended?
options: Participation is the binding constraint on whether reduce/recycle systems actually work
Bins sort waste automatically regardless of behaviour
Education removes the need for any infrastructure
Public attitudes have no measurable effect on recycling outcomes
optionExplanations: Correct: even good infrastructure fails without behaviour change, so building participation is a long-term enabler.
Wrong: sorting depends on people, not the bins themselves.
Wrong: education complements but cannot replace collection and processing infrastructure.
Wrong: participation rates strongly determine real recycling performance.

hard.question: Education spending yields no metric gain this year. On what basis is it still rational?
options: It builds future participation, the binding constraint on whether recovery systems succeed
It substitutes entirely for collection infrastructure
It produces immediate measurable recycling gains
Attitudes are irrelevant to system performance
optionExplanations: Correct: it is a long-term investment in the factor that limits recovery.
Wrong: it complements, not replaces, infrastructure.
Wrong: that contradicts the premise of a lag.
Wrong: attitudes strongly shape outcomes.

easy.question: Why is old electronics ('e-waste') special?
options: It has valuable metals and toxic parts
It is food waste
It is harmless and rots away
It has no value
optionExplanations: Right: it holds recoverable metals and toxics.
Wrong: it isn't food waste.
Wrong: it's toxic and non-biodegradable.
Wrong: its metals have real value.

standard.question: Why should e-waste be treated as a resource stream rather than ordinary rubbish?
options: It contains recoverable critical materials and hazardous substances that must be contained
It is biodegradable and harmless if buried
It cannot be recycled by any known method
It has no economic value
optionExplanations: Correct: e-waste holds valuable critical/rare-earth materials and toxics, so recovery captures value and prevents contamination.
Wrong: it is non-biodegradable and leaches heavy metals.
Wrong: established 'urban mining' processes recover its materials.
Wrong: the recoverable metals give it real economic value.

hard.question: Why is e-waste described as simultaneously a hazard and an opportunity?
options: It contains toxic substances needing containment and critical materials worth recovering
It is biodegradable yet valuable
It is harmless but worthless
It is both impossible to recycle and toxic
optionExplanations: Correct: the dual nature — risk and resource — defines how it should be handled.
Wrong: it is not biodegradable.
Wrong: it is hazardous and valuable, not harmless/worthless.
Wrong: it is recyclable via urban mining.

easy.question: Why can't a city just keep building new tips?
options: Land runs out and tips pollute
Land is unlimited
Tips are free forever
Tips recycle the waste
optionExplanations: Right: land is finite and tips cause pollution.
Wrong: urban land is scarce.
Wrong: tips have ongoing costs.
Wrong: tips store, not recycle.

standard.question: Why is responding to a full landfill by simply opening another one regarded as an unsustainable 'trap' rather than a solution?
options: Land is finite and every new site reproduces lasting leachate, methane and land-opportunity costs
Landfills can be operated indefinitely at no environmental cost
Suitable land near cities is effectively unlimited
Landfills recycle the waste they receive
optionExplanations: Correct: it postpones the problem while consuming scarce land and locking in long-term pollution liabilities.
Wrong: landfills carry ongoing leachate, gas and monitoring costs.
Wrong: peri-urban land is scarce and contested, which is the core constraint.
Wrong: landfills store waste; they do not recycle it.

hard.question: Why is 'build another landfill' described as deferring rather than solving the problem?
options: It consumes finite land and creates long-lived liabilities without reducing waste generation
Landfills permanently neutralise the waste they receive
Land near cities is effectively abundant
It eliminates the need for collection
optionExplanations: Correct: it postpones the issue while adding liabilities and using scarce land.
Wrong: landfills store, not neutralise.
Wrong: peri-urban land is scarce.
Wrong: collection is still required.

easy.question: What makes a proper landfill safer than an open dump?
options: Lining and controls that stop pollution leaking
Only its name
It is bigger
It takes more waste daily
optionExplanations: Right: engineering contains the pollution.
Wrong: it's not just a name.
Wrong: size isn't the point.
Wrong: throughput isn't the point.

standard.question: What fundamentally distinguishes an engineered landfill from an open dump?
options: Containment engineering — lining, leachate treatment and gas control that limit contamination
Only the official name authorities give the site
Open dumps always cover a larger area
Engineered landfills simply accept more waste per day
optionExplanations: Correct: the defining difference is the engineered barriers and treatment that contain pollution.
Wrong: it is an engineering distinction, not a naming one.
Wrong: size is not the distinguishing feature.
Wrong: throughput is not what makes a site 'engineered'.

hard.question: Evaluating the cheapest disposal route, what is the central hidden cost of an open dump?
options: Externalised health and environmental damage borne mainly by nearby poor communities
Higher upfront capital than engineered sites
Lower daily capacity than a sealed tip
Excessive recycling output
optionExplanations: Correct: the 'cheapness' relies on costs being shifted onto the vulnerable.
Wrong: open dumps are cheaper up front, not dearer.
Wrong: capacity is not the hidden cost.
Wrong: dumps do not boost recycling.

easy.question: Why line the bottom of a landfill?
options: To stop toxic liquid reaching water
To make it look neat
To speed up rotting
No real reason
optionExplanations: Right: lining keeps leachate out of water.
Wrong: looks aren't the function.
Wrong: it contains liquid, not speeds decay.
Wrong: it has a clear purpose.

standard.question: Why is impermeable lining MOST critical when a landfill sits above or near an aquifer?
options: Leachate can percolate into groundwater that communities rely on for drinking water
Lining mainly makes the site look tidier
Lining speeds up decomposition of the waste
The presence of an aquifer is irrelevant to landfill design
optionExplanations: Correct: without a barrier, toxic leachate reaches the aquifer, directly threatening water supplies.
Wrong: appearance is not the function of lining.
Wrong: lining contains liquids; it does not accelerate decomposition.
Wrong: an underlying aquifer is precisely what makes containment essential.

hard.question: Why is full lining and leachate treatment most justified above an aquifer, despite the cost?
options: Contaminating a drinking-water aquifer is largely irreversible and high-consequence
Lining mainly improves the site's appearance
Aquifers accelerate waste decomposition
Cost is the only relevant factor
optionExplanations: Correct: irreversibility and high stakes justify the expense.
Wrong: appearance is not the rationale.
Wrong: aquifers do not speed decay.
Wrong: consequence, not just cost, drives the decision.

easy.question: Why isn't sending waste to a faraway town a real fix?
options: The waste and harm still exist, just elsewhere
It destroys the waste
It is always cheapest
Towns want the waste
optionExplanations: Right: it relocates the burden, not removes it.
Wrong: relocation isn't destruction.
Wrong: long hauls add cost.
Wrong: communities increasingly refuse it.

standard.question: Why is trucking waste to a distant rural 'hinterland' criticised as a false solution?
options: It displaces the environmental burden onto another community without reducing total waste
It permanently destroys the waste
It is always the cheapest option available
It eliminates transport emissions
optionExplanations: Correct: the waste and its impacts still exist — they are merely relocated onto people with less power.
Wrong: relocation is not destruction; the waste persists.
Wrong: long hauls add fuel and compensation costs.
Wrong: longer transport increases, not eliminates, emissions.

hard.question: The 'waste hinterland' concept is best used to challenge which assumption?
options: That waste exported out of sight has genuinely been dealt with
That recycling is unprofitable
That cities have no rural links
That transport is emission-free
optionExplanations: Correct: it exposes 'out of sight, out of mind' as a false resolution.
Wrong: not its central point.
Wrong: it assumes such links exist.
Wrong: a secondary issue, not the core assumption.

easy.question: What is 'leachate'?
options: Toxic liquid that drips from rotting rubbish
Clean drinking water
A type of gas
Recycled water
optionExplanations: Right: it's contaminated liquid from waste.
Wrong: it's polluted, not clean.
Wrong: that's the gas (methane).
Wrong: it isn't treated water.

standard.question: Which definition of 'leachate' is correct, and why is it hazardous?
options: Liquid that percolates through waste, carrying dissolved contaminants and heavy metals into soil and water
Recycled water produced by a treatment plant
A gaseous form of methane released by landfills
Clean rainwater runoff from a capped site
optionExplanations: Correct: rainwater passing through waste dissolves toxins, which then migrate into soil and groundwater.
Wrong: leachate is contaminated, not treated, water.
Wrong: leachate is a liquid; methane is the gaseous emission.
Wrong: it is contaminated, not clean, runoff.

hard.question: Why is leachate a particularly insidious pollutant?
options: It silently migrates through soil into groundwater, with health effects appearing only later
It is immediately visible and easily contained
It evaporates harmlessly
It only affects surface aesthetics
optionExplanations: Correct: delayed, hidden contamination makes it hard to detect and address in time.
Wrong: it is often invisible and mobile.
Wrong: it persists in soil and water.
Wrong: it is a serious health hazard.

easy.question: Why is the gas from rotting rubbish a problem?
options: It warms the planet and can catch fire
It cools the air
It is harmless
Rubbish makes no gas
optionExplanations: Right: methane is flammable and warms the climate.
Wrong: it warms, not cools.
Wrong: it's hazardous.
Wrong: rotting waste makes lots of gas.

standard.question: Why is uncaptured landfill methane a particular climate priority compared with the CO2 from the same waste?
options: Methane has a far higher short-term global-warming potential than CO2
Methane cools the atmosphere
Landfills emit no methane
Methane becomes harmless immediately on release
optionExplanations: Correct: per tonne, methane traps far more heat over the short term, so capturing it yields outsized climate benefit.
Wrong: methane is a potent warming gas, not a coolant.
Wrong: anaerobic decay in landfills is a major methane source.
Wrong: it remains a powerful greenhouse gas once emitted.

hard.question: Capturing landfill methane is often prioritised over flaring. Why?
options: Capture both prevents potent emissions and recovers usable energy
Flaring produces more energy than capture
Methane has no climate effect
Capture increases explosion risk
optionExplanations: Correct: capture delivers a climate and an energy benefit at once.
Wrong: flaring wastes the energy.
Wrong: methane is a potent GHG.
Wrong: capture reduces, not raises, risk.

easy.question: Why does driving rubbish far away matter?
options: It burns more fuel and pollutes more
Distance doesn't matter
It boosts recycling
Trucks have no exhaust
optionExplanations: Right: more fuel means more emissions and cost.
Wrong: distance adds real cost.
Wrong: it doesn't help recycling.
Wrong: trucks emit exhaust.

standard.question: Why does the distance waste is hauled matter for sustainability, not just for the budget?
options: Longer hauls burn more fuel, adding emissions to the waste system's ecological footprint
Hauling distance has no environmental consequence
Longer transport raises recycling rates
Collection vehicles produce no emissions
optionExplanations: Correct: transport fuel use is part of the system's footprint, so distance has a real environmental cost.
Wrong: fuel combustion makes distance environmentally significant.
Wrong: distance does not improve recycling.
Wrong: diesel collection fleets are a notable emissions source.

hard.question: Why does long-distance hauling weaken the case for centralised disposal?
options: Transport fuel adds emissions and cost that scale with distance, enlarging the footprint
Distance has no bearing on emissions
Centralisation always lowers the footprint
Hauling improves recycling quality
optionExplanations: Correct: haul distance is a real environmental and financial cost of centralisation.
Wrong: fuel use scales with distance.
Wrong: centralisation can raise transport impacts.
Wrong: hauling does not improve recycling.

easy.question: What can an old, sealed tip become?
options: A park or playing field
Never anything
Only dense housing
A new dump
optionExplanations: Right: capped sites become green amenity.
Wrong: reuse is common.
Wrong: housing on a tip is risky.
Wrong: reuse retires the dump.

standard.question: How can decommissioned landfill land be returned to safe public use?
options: A properly sealed and capped site, once stabilised, can host parks or playing fields
Such land can never be reused for any purpose
It is only ever safe for dense housing
It must immediately be reopened as a new dump
optionExplanations: Correct: with capping and gas/leachate management, former landfills are commonly converted to green amenity.
Wrong: managed reuse is well established, so 'never' is false.
Wrong: housing directly on a former tip raises contamination risk; open amenity is safer.
Wrong: reuse aims to retire the site, not perpetuate dumping.

hard.question: What condition makes converting a former landfill into parkland safe?
options: Proper capping plus ongoing gas and leachate management once the site has stabilised
Immediate construction before stabilisation
Removing all monitoring systems
Building housing directly on the waste
optionExplanations: Correct: stabilisation and continued management are prerequisites for safe reuse.
Wrong: building before stabilisation is unsafe.
Wrong: monitoring must continue.
Wrong: housing on waste raises contamination risk.

easy.question: Why might charging for disposal cause illegal dumping?
options: People dodge fees if there's no easy legal option
Fees always cut waste
Dumping helps recycling
Fines cost nothing
optionExplanations: Right: charges backfire without easy alternatives.
Wrong: fees can shift behaviour to dumping.
Wrong: fly-tipping isn't recycling.
Wrong: enforcement has costs.

standard.question: Disposal charges sometimes increase illegal dumping. What does this reveal about policy design?
options: Price signals backfire unless paired with an affordable, convenient legal alternative
Higher fees always reduce total waste generated
Illegal dumping improves overall recycling
Enforcement is costless to administer
optionExplanations: Correct: charging without an easy lawful route pushes behaviour toward evasion — incentives must be designed holistically.
Wrong: fees can shift behaviour to dumping rather than reducing waste.
Wrong: fly-tipped waste is unsorted and harmful, not a recycling gain.
Wrong: monitoring and prosecution carry real costs.

hard.question: Rising disposal fees increase fly-tipping. What design principle does this reveal?
options: Incentives must be paired with accessible legal alternatives or they distort behaviour
Higher prices always reduce the targeted behaviour
Enforcement alone is sufficient and costless
Fees have no behavioural effect
optionExplanations: Correct: pricing without an easy lawful route diverts behaviour to evasion.
Wrong: prices can shift behaviour the wrong way.
Wrong: enforcement is costly and partial.
Wrong: fees clearly change behaviour.

easy.question: What is 'urban mining'?
options: Recovering materials from old waste
Digging new mines in cities
Building tips underground
Burning waste
optionExplanations: Right: it reclaims materials from waste.
Wrong: it's not virgin mining.
Wrong: it's recovery, not disposal.
Wrong: it isn't burning.

standard.question: What is 'urban mining', and why is it considered circular?
options: Recovering materials (and land) from waste or old landfills, returning resources to productive use
Opening new mineral mines within city limits
Building landfills underground to save surface space
A specialised high-temperature form of incineration
optionExplanations: Correct: it reclaims resources already in the waste stock, feeding them back into the economy.
Wrong: it mines waste, not virgin geology.
Wrong: it is about recovery, not subterranean disposal.
Wrong: it is materials recovery, not combustion.

hard.question: Why is landfill mining considered circular despite its cost and disruption?
options: It returns buried materials and land to productive use rather than abandoning them
It opens new virgin mineral mines
It buries waste more deeply
It is a form of incineration
optionExplanations: Correct: reclaiming stocked resources embodies circular thinking.
Wrong: it mines waste, not geology.
Wrong: it recovers, not re-buries.
Wrong: it is recovery, not combustion.

easy.question: What is a downside of burning rubbish for energy?
options: It pollutes the air
It has no downsides
It is always the cheapest
It needs no waste
optionExplanations: Right: combustion releases air pollution.
Wrong: it has real downsides.
Wrong: it's capital-intensive.
Wrong: it needs waste to burn.

standard.question: Evaluating incineration, which statement BEST captures its central sustainability trade-off?
options: It sharply cuts waste volume and recovers energy, but emits air pollutants and provokes community opposition
It is a cost-free, impact-free method of disposal
It is always cheaper than recycling
It removes the need for any waste collection
optionExplanations: Correct: the gains (volume reduction, energy) are real but come with emissions, ash and NIMBY/governance costs.
Wrong: it has high capital costs and genuine emissions.
Wrong: cost depends on context; it is capital-intensive and not always cheapest.
Wrong: waste must still be collected and delivered to the plant.

hard.question: To what extent does incineration align with sustainable development?
options: Partially — it recovers energy and land but imposes emissions, ash and equity costs
Fully — it is the most sustainable option available
Not at all — it offers no benefits
It is identical to recycling in its impacts
optionExplanations: Correct: a balanced 'partial' judgement captures both the gains and the costs.
Wrong: it sits low on the hierarchy, not top.
Wrong: it does provide energy and volume reduction.
Wrong: recycling avoids combustion emissions.

easy.question: Why does public trust matter for building waste facilities?
options: People must accept them for projects to go ahead
It doesn't matter
Trust lowers recycling
Protests don't count
optionExplanations: Right: consent is needed to site and run them.
Wrong: trust is a real constraint.
Wrong: trust raises participation.
Wrong: opposition shapes outcomes.

standard.question: Why does public trust materially affect what waste infrastructure a city can actually build?
options: Siting, planning consent and ongoing participation all depend on community acceptance
Trust becomes irrelevant once a project is funded
High public trust reduces recycling participation
Protest movements carry no legal or political weight
optionExplanations: Correct: without consent, projects are blocked, delayed or under-used, so trust is a real constraint on options.
Wrong: funding does not override planning objections and operational cooperation.
Wrong: trust tends to raise, not lower, participation.
Wrong: organised opposition routinely shapes planning outcomes.

hard.question: NIMBY opposition is best interpreted as evidence that...
options: public consent is a genuine constraint on what infrastructure is feasible
protest has no effect on planning
trust is irrelevant once a project is funded
communities oppose every project regardless
optionExplanations: Correct: feasibility depends on social acceptance, not just finance.
Wrong: opposition shapes outcomes.
Wrong: trust still governs consent and operation.
Wrong: an overgeneralisation.

easy.question: A risk of letting a private firm run the incinerator?
options: They may cut corners on pollution control
It becomes pollution-free
It costs the city nothing
It makes zero waste
optionExplanations: Right: profit motives can weaken standards.
Wrong: emissions remain.
Wrong: oversight still costs.
Wrong: ash and emissions remain.

standard.question: What is the principal governance risk of privatising a waste-to-energy plant?
options: A profit motive can weaken adherence to emissions and health standards unless tightly regulated
Private operation guarantees zero emissions
It removes all costs from the city
It automatically delivers zero waste
optionExplanations: Correct: cost-minimising operators may cut corners on pollution control without strong oversight.
Wrong: ownership does not eliminate the inherent emissions of combustion.
Wrong: the city still bears contract and oversight costs.
Wrong: incineration is not zero waste; ash and emissions remain.

hard.question: Which safeguard most directly addresses the risk of privatised incineration?
options: Strong, independently enforced emissions and health regulation
Allowing the operator to self-report freely
Removing public oversight to cut costs
Guaranteeing the operator maximum profit
optionExplanations: Correct: independent regulation counters the profit-driven incentive to cut corners.
Wrong: self-reporting invites the very risk.
Wrong: oversight is the safeguard, not its removal.
Wrong: that worsens the incentive problem.

easy.question: Why fit filters ('scrubbers') on an incinerator?
options: To cut harmful pollution
To make more ash
To increase smoke
No reason
optionExplanations: Right: scrubbers reduce toxic emissions.
Wrong: they don't add ash.
Wrong: they cut, not add, pollution.
Wrong: they protect health.

standard.question: Calling incineration 'waste of energy' rather than 'waste to energy' is a warning that...
options: uncontrolled emissions and health costs can outweigh the energy actually recovered
incinerators never generate any usable energy
energy recovery is purely a marketing invention
burning always wastes 100% of the available energy
optionExplanations: Correct: if pollution control is poor, the social and health costs can exceed the value of the energy.
Wrong: they do generate usable energy — the issue is net cost-benefit.
Wrong: energy recovery is real, not invented.

hard.question: The 'waste of energy' critique implies incineration should be judged by...
options: net impact — energy recovered minus health and environmental costs
energy output alone
capital cost alone
the number of homes powered only
optionExplanations: Correct: a net cost-benefit framing is the point of the critique.
Wrong: output alone ignores the costs.
Wrong: cost alone ignores the benefits.
Wrong: a single headline metric is insufficient.

easy.question: Why doesn't burning make waste 'disappear'?
options: It leaves ash and gases to deal with
It vanishes completely
Ash is always safe
No leftovers at all
optionExplanations: Right: combustion leaves residues and emissions.
Wrong: there's always a residue.
Wrong: fly ash can be toxic.
Wrong: by-products are unavoidable.

standard.question: Why is it inaccurate to claim incineration makes waste 'disappear'?
options: It converts waste into ash and flue gases that still require careful management
It fully converts waste into clean energy with no residue
The ash it produces is always inert and harmless
It leaves no by-products whatsoever
optionExplanations: Correct: combustion transforms waste into residues (often toxic ash) plus emissions, not nothing.
Wrong: there is always a residual stream.
Wrong: fly ash in particular can be hazardous.
Wrong: ash and gases are unavoidable by-products.

hard.question: Why does incineration not remove the need for landfill?
options: It produces toxic ash and residues that still require safe disposal
It fully converts waste into energy
Its ash is always reusable in construction
It leaves no residue at all
optionExplanations: Correct: residual (often hazardous) ash still needs containment.
Wrong: there is always a residue.
Wrong: ash reuse needs testing and is not guaranteed.
Wrong: residue is unavoidable.

easy.question: How does selling the electricity help?
options: It earns money to offset costs
It removes pollution
It changes nothing
It makes more ash
optionExplanations: Right: revenue improves the cost balance.
Wrong: it doesn't end emissions.
Wrong: it affects economics.
Wrong: energy sales don't raise ash.

standard.question: How does energy recovery change the overall assessment of incineration?
options: Revenue and displaced fossil energy partially offset its costs and emissions
It removes all pollution from the process
It is irrelevant to the plant's economics
It increases the volume of ash produced
optionExplanations: Correct: selling power and avoiding fossil generation improve the cost-benefit balance, though not the local pollution.
Wrong: recovering energy does not eliminate stack emissions.
Wrong: energy sales are central to incineration economics.
Wrong: energy recovery does not raise ash volumes.

hard.question: How should energy revenue change the evaluation of an incinerator?
options: It improves the cost-benefit balance but does not offset local pollution
It makes the plant pollution-free
It is irrelevant to the evaluation
It increases the toxic ash produced
optionExplanations: Correct: revenue helps the economics but not the air-quality ledger.
Wrong: emissions persist.
Wrong: revenue is central to evaluation.
Wrong: energy sales do not raise ash.

easy.question: Why limit incinerator emissions by law?
options: The gases harm health
To raise electricity prices
They are harmless
To cut recycling
optionExplanations: Right: the pollutants are harmful.
Wrong: it's about health, not pricing.
Wrong: they're the concern.
Wrong: caps target air, not recycling.

standard.question: Why do regulators cap incinerator emissions despite the energy benefit?
options: Combustion releases dioxins, particulates and acid gases that harm human health

Incinerator emissions are biologically harmless
Caps are intended to suppress recycling
optionExplanations: Correct: the pollutants are genuinely harmful, so limits protect public health.

Wrong: the emissions are precisely the health concern.
Wrong: caps target air quality, not recycling.

hard.question: A city meets emissions caps by buying credits rather than upgrading. What is the limitation?
options: Credits satisfy the rule on paper but do not cut the city's own harmful emissions

Upgrading is always cheaper than credits
Credits directly improve local air quality
optionExplanations: Correct: paper compliance leaves local pollution unchanged.

Wrong: credits do not clean local air.

easy.question: Why might a city move away from burning over time?

Burning is banned everywhere
It makes too much energy
Burning is free
optionExplanations: Right: the hierarchy favours recovery over burning.
Wrong: incineration is legal.

standard.question: Why might a city plan to move beyond incineration over time, even with a functioning plant?
options: Reduction and recycling sit higher on the hierarchy and avoid combustion emissions altogether
Incineration is legally banned everywhere
Incinerators generate too much energy to be useful
Burning waste is entirely free of cost
optionExplanations: Correct: the hierarchy favours preventing and recovering materials over burning them.

Wrong: incineration is capital- and emission-intensive, not free.

hard.question: Why might retiring a working incinerator be defensible despite lost capacity?
options: Shifting to higher-hierarchy options avoids ongoing emissions and aligns with long-term goals
Incineration is universally illegal
Energy output is a disadvantage
Burning incurs no costs
optionExplanations: Correct: long-term hierarchy gains can outweigh short-term capacity loss.
Wrong: it remains legal.

It burns more rubbish
Wasting heat is greener

standard.question: Why is recovering an incinerator's waste heat considered 'more circular' than venting it?
